name: Andorra
tagline: Seven centuries of survival in the high Pyrenees, ruled by two princes and never conquered.
summary: Andorra is one of Europe's oldest surviving states, a cluster of valleys high in the eastern Pyrenees that has outlasted empires by belonging, in a sense, to no one. Legend traces its origin to Charlemagne, who is said to have granted the valleys their charter after his armies passed through against the Moors, a memory still sung in the national anthem 'El Gran Carlemany'. Its real political identity was forged in 1278, when a long feudal quarrel between the Bishop of Urgell and the Count of Foix was settled by a charter, the paréage, that placed Andorra under their joint suzerainty. From that compromise grew a unique co-principality whose two heads of state today are the President of the French Republic and the Bishop of Urgell, a Spanish prelate. For more than seven hundred years this divided overlordship, together with the country's isolation and a symbolic annual tribute, preserved Andorran neutrality and self-government through Europe's wars. In 1993 Andorra adopted its first written constitution, becoming a sovereign parliamentary democracy with the co-princes reduced to constitutional heads of state, and that same year it joined the United Nations.

founding.label: The Paréage of 1278 and the birth of the co-principality
founding.detail: Andorra's distinctive statehood was created not by conquest or unification but by a feudal compromise. A long dispute over the valleys between the Bishop of Urgell, on the Spanish side of the Pyrenees, and the Count of Foix, on the French side, was settled in 1278 by a charter known as the paréage, signed at Lleida on 8 September. It placed Andorra under the joint suzerainty of the two lords, each receiving an annual token tribute, and a second paréage in 1288 fixed the details. Together these documents became Andorra's first basic law and the foundation of its sovereignty for the next seven centuries. The arrangement produced a co-principality in which neither lord could absorb the territory, an enduring example of a successful diarchy that protected Andorran autonomy until a modern constitution was adopted in 1993.

quickFacts.label: Legendary founder
quickFacts.value: Charlemagne, in the tradition sung in the anthem 'El Gran Carlemany'

quickFacts.label: Co-principality created
quickFacts.value: The paréage of 1278 (confirmed 1288) under the Bishop of Urgell and Count of Foix

quickFacts.label: Heads of state today
quickFacts.value: Two co-princes: the President of France and the Bishop of Urgell (Spain)

quickFacts.label: Capital
quickFacts.value: Andorra la Vella, the highest capital in Europe

quickFacts.label: United Nations
quickFacts.value: Admitted on 28 July 1993; joined the Council of Europe in 1994

eras.title: Charlemagne, the Marca Hispanica and the Bishops of Urgell
eras.standfirst: From a frontier of the Carolingian world, the high valleys passed under the lordship of the Bishop of Urgell, where the seeds of Andorra's separate identity were sown.
body: Andorra's beginnings lie in legend and in the politics of the early medieval frontier. The Andorran tradition, preserved in the national anthem 'El Gran Carlemany', holds that the emperor Charlemagne granted the valleys their first charter after his Frankish armies pushed across the eastern Pyrenees against the Moors at the end of the eighth century. Whatever its precise truth, the region did lie within the Marca Hispanica, the chain of buffer counties Charlemagne and his successors established south of the mountains to shield the Carolingian realm, and Christianity and Carolingian administration were knit into the valleys in this period.

Out of the Carolingian order emerged the lasting power that would shape Andorra: the Church. The county of Urgell, on the southern slope of the Pyrenees, and especially its bishops at La Seu d'Urgell, accumulated lands and rights in the valleys. By the late eleventh and early twelfth centuries the Bishop of Urgell held lordship over Andorra, often defending it with the help of secular protectors drawn from the Pyrenean nobility. The valleys, remote and hard to reach, developed their own customs of communal land use and pasturage that would prove remarkably durable.

To secure the valleys against rival claims, the bishops entrusted their defence to powerful neighbours. The counts of the house of Foix, on the French side of the range, acquired the role of the bishop's protectors and, through it, a claim of their own over Andorra. By the early twelfth century the two interests, the spiritual lordship of the Bishop of Urgell and the secular ambitions of the counts, were both rooted in the same small territory. The collision of those claims would, more than a century later, give Andorra the unique constitution it still keeps.

events.title: The Charlemagne tradition and the Marca Hispanica
events.summary: Andorran tradition, recalled in the anthem 'El Gran Carlemany', credits Charlemagne with freeing the valleys from the Moors and granting their charter; the region lay within the Carolingian frontier zone of the Marca Hispanica.

events.title: Christianity and Carolingian order
events.summary: As part of the Carolingian frontier, the valleys were integrated into Latin Christendom, with the church of Urgell emerging as the dominant local authority over the centuries that followed.

events.title: Lordship of the Bishop of Urgell
events.summary: The Bishop of Urgell consolidated lordship over the Andorran valleys, entrusting their defence to secular protectors, among them the powerful counts of Foix.

figures.name: Charlemagne
figures.role: Emperor of the Franks, legendary founder of Andorra
figures.blurb: In Andorran tradition Charlemagne liberated the valleys from the Moors and granted their first charter, a memory enshrined in the national anthem. Historically, his frontier zone, the Marca Hispanica, drew the region into Carolingian Christendom.

figures.name: The Bishops of Urgell
figures.blurb: From their see at La Seu d'Urgell the bishops accumulated lands and rights over Andorra, becoming its dominant lords. Their successors remain co-princes of Andorra to this day, the only such office held by a serving bishop.

pullquote.text: Andorran tradition holds that Charlemagne granted the valleys their charter, a memory the country still sings in its national anthem.
pullquote.attribution: On the legendary founding of Andorra

eras.title: The Paréage of 1278 and the Making of the Co-Principality
eras.standfirst: A feudal quarrel between bishop and count was settled by a charter of joint sovereignty, giving Andorra the divided overlordship that has defined it ever since.
body: By the thirteenth century the rival claims of the Bishop of Urgell and the Count of Foix had hardened into armed dispute over the Andorran valleys. Rather than let either side win outright, the quarrel was mediated and settled in 1278 by a feudal charter, the paréage, signed at Lleida on 8 September. It provided that sovereignty over Andorra be shared between the bishop and the count, with each acknowledging the other's rights; a second paréage in 1288 settled outstanding details of justice and defence. These two acts gave Andorra its territory, its political form and the principle of joint, indivisible suzerainty.

The genius of the arrangement lay in its balance. Because neither co-prince could rule alone or dispossess the other, the small territory between them could be neither annexed nor easily coerced, and the Andorrans themselves gained room to manage their own affairs. In return for protection, Andorra rendered to its two lords a symbolic annual tribute, the questia, an act of homage rather than a burden. The same charters were understood to recognise Andorra's neutrality, a status the valleys would guard through the wars of the centuries to come.

The paréages remained the unaltered constitutional bedrock of Andorra until 1993, the longest-lived such settlement in Europe. They also set Andorra on a path distinct from the emerging nation-states around it: while France and Spain consolidated into centralised kingdoms, Andorra preserved a medieval, contractual sovereignty divided between a French secular lord and a Spanish prelate, an arrangement that would survive every transformation of its two great neighbours.

events.title: The first paréage
events.summary: A feudal charter signed at Lleida ended the dispute between the Bishop of Urgell and the Count of Foix by placing Andorra under their joint suzerainty, creating the co-principality.

events.title: The second paréage
events.summary: A second charter settled questions of justice and defence left open in 1278; together the two paréages formed Andorra's first basic law and constitutional foundation until 1993.

events.title: The questia tribute and neutrality
events.summary: Andorra rendered a symbolic annual tribute, the questia, to each co-prince as an act of homage; the settlement was understood to recognise the valleys' neutrality.

events.title: Self-government within divided lordship
events.summary: Because neither co-prince could rule alone, Andorrans gained latitude to govern their own valleys, developing assemblies of the parishes that prefigured a national council.

figures.name: Pere d'Urtx
figures.role: Bishop of Urgell, co-signatory of the paréage
figures.blurb: As Bishop of Urgell, Pere d'Urtx was a party to the paréage of 1278 that established joint sovereignty over Andorra with the Count of Foix, fixing the ecclesiastical half of the co-principality that endures today.

figures.name: Roger-Bernard III, Count of Foix
figures.blurb: Count of Foix at the time of the 1278 settlement, he secured for his house the secular share of Andorran sovereignty. That claim later passed through the kings of Navarre to the crown, and then the presidency, of France.

pullquote.text: Placed under the joint suzerainty of the French counts of Foix and the Spanish bishops of Urgell in 1278, this unique diarchical arrangement has persisted to the present.

eras.title: Two Princes, Neutrality and Endurance
eras.standfirst: The valleys won their own council, the French half of the co-principality passed to the kings and then head of state of France, and Andorra slipped quietly through Europe's wars.
body: In 1419 the co-princes permitted the Andorrans to form their own assembly, the Consell de la Terra, the Council of the Land, drawn from the heads of the leading households of the parishes. One of the oldest representative bodies in Europe, it gave the valleys a standing voice in their own administration and justice, meeting in time at the Casa de la Vall in Andorra la Vella. Beneath the formal overlordship of bishop and count, Andorra ran much of its own affairs, a self-governing community of mountain parishes.

The secular half of the co-principality drifted northward into the highest reaches of French power. The rights of the counts of Foix passed by inheritance to the kings of Navarre, and when Henry of Navarre became Henry IV of France he carried the title with him; an edict of 1607 confirmed the King of France and the Bishop of Urgell as Andorra's two co-princes. Through the French Revolution, the abolition and restoration of the monarchy, and the succession of French republics and empires, the French co-princely role was simply transferred to whoever was head of the French state, so that today it is held by the President of the Republic.

Andorra's value to its powerful neighbours lay precisely in its smallness and neutrality, and the country cultivated both. Sheltered by its mountains and by the rule that neither prince could act alone, it stayed out of the wars that repeatedly convulsed France and Spain. The tiny principality drew its modest livelihood from pasturage, tobacco and the trade, licit and otherwise, that crossed its passes, while guarding the medieval liberties the paréages had granted. The endurance of so small a state amid great powers became its defining achievement.

events.title: Founding of the Consell de la Terra
events.summary: The co-princes allowed Andorrans to create the Council of the Land, drawn from heads of households of the parishes, one of Europe's oldest representative assemblies and the ancestor of today's parliament.

events.title: The French crown becomes co-prince
events.summary: After the rights of Foix passed through Navarre to Henry IV, an edict confirmed the King of France and the Bishop of Urgell as Andorra's co-princes; the French role later passed to the head of state.

events.title: The Casa de la Vall
events.summary: The Andorran council established its seat and court at the Casa de la Vall in Andorra la Vella, long the symbolic home of the valleys' self-government.

events.title: Neutrality through revolution and war
events.summary: Through the French Revolution and Napoleonic Wars the co-principality survived; the French co-princely role transferred to each new French regime while Andorra guarded its neutrality.

figures.name: Henry IV of France
figures.role: King of France and Navarre, co-prince of Andorra
figures.blurb: Inheriting the Foix claim through the kingdom of Navarre, Henry IV brought the secular co-principality of Andorra into the French crown. His 1607 edict fixed the King of France and the Bishop of Urgell as co-princes.

pullquote.text: Andorra was governed jointly by representatives of the Spanish bishop of Urgell and of the French head of state, each of whom received an annual payment of a token tribute.

eras.title: Reform, a Brief 'King', and the Strains of the Old Order
eras.standfirst: The feudal co-principality was modernised by stages, weathered the bizarre episode of a self-proclaimed king, and slowly outgrew a constitution written for the Middle Ages.
body: By the nineteenth century the medieval order was straining against the modern world. In 1866 a reform led by the Andorran syndic Guillem d'Areny-Plandolit, and decreed by the episcopal co-prince, broadened the Council, regularised its elections and confirmed the franchise for the heads of households, laying down many of the symbols and institutions of modern Andorra. It was a cautious modernisation that left the co-principality intact but began the long shift from feudal lordship toward representative government.

The fragility of so antique a state was exposed in 1934 by one of the strangest episodes in European history. Amid local agitation over voting rights and reform, a Russian-born adventurer, Boris Skossyreff, persuaded the Andorran council to proclaim him King Boris I; his reign lasted barely a fortnight before he was arrested by Spanish authorities and expelled. The farce underscored how exposed and undefined Andorra's status had become, dependent on the goodwill of its co-princes and lacking the institutions of a modern state. Andorra's anomalies extended even to war: having declared war on Germany in 1914 without fighting, it was reportedly left out of the peace and remained nominally at war until a separate declaration of peace decades later.

Through the twentieth century Andorra remained neutral, sitting out both world wars as it had sat out earlier European conflicts, and serving in the Second World War as a quiet crossing-point in the Pyrenees. But its medieval constitution increasingly fitted poorly with a country being transformed by roads, tourism and banking. Andorrans had no clear citizenship rights, no political parties, and a government answerable to two foreign heads of state. Pressure mounted, through the post-war decades, for a written constitution that would turn the ancient co-principality into a modern, sovereign democracy.

events.title: The New Reform
events.summary: A reform led by syndic Guillem d'Areny-Plandolit and decreed by the episcopal co-prince broadened the Council and regularised elections, modernising Andorra's institutions while keeping the co-principality.

events.title: Neutral through the First World War
events.summary: Andorra remained outside the fighting of the Great War; an anomaly of its undefined status was that it was reportedly omitted from the peace settlement and stayed nominally at war for decades.

events.title: Boris Skossyreff, 'King Boris I'
events.summary: A Russian-born adventurer briefly induced the Andorran council to proclaim him king during a period of unrest over voting rights; he was arrested and expelled within about two weeks.

events.title: Neutral in the Second World War
events.summary: Andorra preserved its neutrality through the Second World War, its mountain passes serving as a quiet crossing-point between Vichy France and Spain.

figures.name: Guillem d'Areny-Plandolit
figures.blurb: As syndic, Areny-Plandolit led the New Reform of 1866 that broadened Andorra's Council and regularised its elections, the first major modernisation of the medieval co-principality and a milestone toward representative government.

figures.name: Boris Skossyreff
figures.blurb: A Russian-born adventurer who, during the unrest of the 1930s, persuaded the Andorran council to proclaim him king in July 1934 on a platform of reform and a casino economy. His reign lasted only days before Spanish authorities arrested and expelled him.

pullquote.text: This feudal system of government remained intact until 1993, when a constitution was adopted that greatly reduced the power of the co-princes.

eras.title: A Sovereign Democracy in the Heart of Europe
eras.standfirst: A written constitution turned the medieval co-principality into a parliamentary democracy, the co-princes into constitutional heads of state, and Andorra into a recognised member of the international community.
body: The decisive break with the medieval past came in 1993. On 14 March Andorrans approved by referendum their first written constitution, which established Andorra as a sovereign parliamentary democracy. The co-princes, the President of France and the Bishop of Urgell, were retained as joint heads of state but reduced to a largely symbolic, constitutional role; real executive power passed to a head of government, the cap de govern, with legislative power in the elected General Council and an independent judiciary. The unique diarchy survived, now anchored in popular sovereignty rather than feudal charter.

Sovereignty brought Andorra out of its long isolation and into the world of states. On 28 July 1993, months after adopting its constitution, the principality was admitted to the United Nations, and in 1994 it joined the Council of Europe, binding itself to European standards of democracy and human rights. The country established formal diplomatic relations with partners around the world and took its place, for the first time, as a fully recognised independent state rather than a feudal curiosity.

Modern Andorra lives chiefly from tourism, drawing millions of visitors a year to its ski slopes and duty-free shops, and from a substantial banking and financial sector. Long without a currency of its own, it used the French franc and Spanish peseta and then, by a monetary agreement signed with the European Union on 30 June 2011, adopted the euro as its official currency and won the right to mint its own euro coins. Andorra is not an EU member, but it has moved steadily closer: building on an earlier customs union, it concluded negotiations in 2023 on an association agreement, proposed by the European Commission in 2024, that would give the principality far-reaching access to the EU's single market, the latest chapter in a small nation's long art of survival between greater powers.

Even as it modernised, Andorra took care to guard its mountain heritage. In 2004 UNESCO inscribed the Madriu-Perafita-Claror Valley, a remote glacial landscape covering roughly a tenth of the country, as a World Heritage cultural landscape. It was honoured as a microcosm of how people have worked the high Pyrenees over millennia, preserving a strong mountain culture and a communal system of land ownership reaching back to the thirteenth century, a living link between the valleys of the paréages and the sovereign democracy of today.

events.title: The first written constitution
events.summary: Andorrans approved by referendum a constitution that made Andorra a sovereign parliamentary democracy, with the two co-princes reduced to constitutional heads of state and power vested in an elected government and council.

events.title: Admission to the United Nations
events.summary: Andorra was admitted as a member state of the United Nations, taking its place in the international community as a fully recognised sovereign state.

events.title: Member of the Council of Europe
events.summary: Andorra joined the Council of Europe, committing the new democracy to European standards of human rights and the rule of law.

events.title: Madriu-Perafita-Claror Valley inscribed by UNESCO
events.summary: Andorra's only World Heritage Site, the Madriu-Perafita-Claror Valley, was inscribed as a cultural landscape, recognised for its high-Pyrenean pastoralism and a communal land-ownership system surviving from the 13th century.

events.title: Monetary agreement and the euro
events.summary: Andorra signed a monetary agreement with the European Union allowing it to use the euro as its official currency and to issue its own euro coins.

events.title: Association agreement with the EU
events.summary: After concluding negotiations in 2023, the European Commission in 2024 proposed an association agreement to give Andorra (with San Marino) deep access to the EU single market, comparable to the European Economic Area.

figures.name: Marc Forné Molné
figures.role: Head of Government during the constitutional transition
figures.blurb: Cap de govern through much of the 1990s, Forné led Andorra in the years after the 1993 constitution, guiding the new democracy into the United Nations and the Council of Europe and toward closer ties with Europe.

figures.name: The President of France
figures.blurb: Heir to the rights of the counts of Foix by way of the kings of Navarre and France, the French head of state serves as one of Andorra's two co-princes, an office unique in the world: a republican president who is also a constitutional prince of a foreign state.

pullquote.text: The 1993 constitution established Andorra as a sovereign parliamentary democracy; that same year the principality was admitted to the United Nations.
pullquote.attribution: On Andorra's entry into the modern world of states

figures.name: Charlemagne
figures.blurb: In Andorran tradition the Frankish emperor freed the valleys from the Moors and granted their charter, a founding myth still sung in the national anthem 'El Gran Carlemany'. The region historically lay within his Pyrenean frontier zone.

figures.name: Pere d'Urtx
figures.role: Bishop of Urgell, co-founder of the co-principality
figures.blurb: Bishop of Urgell and a party to the paréage of 1278, which placed Andorra under the joint sovereignty of bishop and count and created the co-principality that survives to this day.

figures.name: Henry IV of France
figures.blurb: Through the kingdom of Navarre he brought the Foix claim over Andorra into the French crown; his 1607 edict fixed the King of France and the Bishop of Urgell as the two co-princes, an arrangement that endures with the French presidency today.

figures.name: Guillem d'Areny-Plandolit
figures.blurb: Architect of the New Reform of 1866 that modernised Andorra's institutions and broadened its Council, beginning the principality's long transition from feudal lordship toward the parliamentary democracy established in 1993.
